results.title: We will produce as much content for machines as for humans: Publicis’ Arthur Sadoun
results.description: New Delhi: Publicis Groupe expects content produced for machines to become as large as its existing human-facing output within three years, as generative search changes how brands are discovered, understood and recommended online. Arthur Sadoun, Chairman and CEO of Publicis Groupe, said the shift would not come at the expense of content created for consumers. Instead, it would expand the overall volume of content that agencies and brands need to produce. “Three years from now, 50% of the content that we are producing today won’t be for humans, but for machines,” Sadoun said during Publicis Groupe’s post-earnings webcast. “We will continue to produce as much content for humans, but we will have to produce a lot of content for machines,” he added. Sadoun was responding to a question on the rise of generative engine optimisation, or GEO, and the need for brands to remain visible as consumers increasingly use AI-powered search and large language models to find information. His forecast does not suggest that conventional advertising, branded films, social content or other consumer-facing communication will decline by half. Publicis instead expects a new layer of content to be created specifically so that AI systems can find, process and use information about brands, products and services. This could include content structured to help generative search platforms understand product attributes, brand authority, availability and relevance when responding to consumer questions. Publicis believes brands will need the right combination of data, technology and production infrastructure to build content for both audiences. “If you have the data, if you have the technology, and in this case, if you have the production backbone, you should be able to see some growth from the fact that not only do we need to talk to humans, but also to machines,” Sadoun said. He described content for machines as part of a new addressable market for Publicis rather than merely another distribution format. Traditional search has largely focused on helping webpages rank against specific queries. Generative systems, however, gather and synthesise information from multiple sources before presenting an answer directly to the user. That means brands must consider not only whether their websites are visible in search results, but also whether AI systems can accurately recognise, interpret and recommend them. Sadoun said visibility alone would not be enough. Brands would still need to connect their investments in AI-facing content with identifiable consumers and measurable commercial outcomes. “There is no way you’re going to be able to talk to machines properly and transform that into sales if you don’t have identity, because at the end of the day, you need an anchor that will allow you to see a person,” he said. “When the LLMs go onto the web to look for something, there is no digital trace of a consumer. So if you don’t have an identity to make sure that you can link your investment to business outcome, you’re basically blind,” Sadoun added. Publicis sees its identity and first-party data capabilities as central to closing that gap. The group has built its marketing model around Epsilon’s identity infrastructure, alongside its media, creative, commerce, CRM, production and technology operations. The argument is that content created for machines must ultimately remain connected to a consumer journey. Without an identity layer, brands may gain visibility in generative responses but struggle to determine whether that visibility leads to consideration, engagement or sales. Publicis is also using the technology and engineering capabilities of Publicis Sapient to support AI-led production and marketing work across the wider group. Sadoun said Sapient’s value extended beyond its standalone technology-consulting revenue because its engineers and enterprise AI capabilities were being deployed across production, media and creative operations. The group’s AI-powered marketing services, which include media, data, creative, commerce, CRM and production, account for 87% of its net revenue and grew organically by 6.5% in the second quarter of 2026. Publicis said production recorded high-single-digit organic growth during the quarter. The group expects growing demand for content that works across human audiences and machine-led discovery to create further opportunities for this business. “The core of our business is marketing transformation. It’s about media, creative, data and technology getting together to transform the marketing model of our clients,” Sadoun said. Publicis’ forecast points to a broader change in brand content strategy. Advertisers may soon need to operate two parallel but connected content systems: one designed to persuade and engage people, and another structured to ensure machines can accurately interpret the brand and surface it in relevant conversations. The expansion would make GEO, first-party identity and content infrastructure increasingly important parts of marketing strategy, rather than specialist extensions of search engine optimisation.

results.title: ViewSonic ViewBoard IN04V-N Brings AI Camera to Collaboration
results.description: ViewSonic Corp., a leading global provider of visual and EdTech solutions, today announced the launch of the ViewBoard IN04V-N Series , its newest premium interactive display. As hybrid collaboration continues to reshape the way organizations and educational institutions communicate, businesses and educators are increasingly seeking integrated solutions that simplify collaboration while delivering engaging experiences. Addressing this growing need, the new ViewBoard combines advanced AI capabilities, enterprise-grade performance, and an integrated 48MP AI camera to support seamless communication across boardrooms, classrooms, training spaces, and collaborative environments. At the heart of the experience is an intelligent imaging system that delivers crystal-clear visuals while keeping presenters naturally in focus through AI facial tracking. Paired with an 8-microphone array, gesture recognition, and sound localization, the display automatically follows speakers, captures voices accurately across the room, and enables touch-free interactions. By integrating these capabilities into a single device, it eliminates the need for external cameras and audio peripherals while creating a more intuitive conferencing experience. Muneer Ahmad, Vice President – Sales & Marketing, ViewSonic India , said: "Video collaboration has become the foundation of modern communication, and organizations today need solutions that are intelligent, seamless, and ready from the moment a meeting begins. With the ViewBoard IN04V-N, we are introducing our first interactive display with a built-in 48MP AI camera that eliminates the need for multiple peripherals while delivering exceptional video quality and intelligent tracking capabilities. Combined with powerful AI tools for collaboration and learning, the IN04V-N is designed to help businesses and educational institutions create smarter, more connected spaces where ideas flow effortlessly and every participant feels fully engaged." Available in 65-inch, 75-inch, and 86-inch screen sizes, the ViewBoard IN04V-N combines enterprise-grade hardware with AI-powered capabilities to simplify collaboration while reducing meeting room complexity. Beyond video conferencing, the new series enhances productivity through an advanced AI toolkit designed to streamline everyday collaboration. The AI Text Recognition Pen instantly converts handwritten notes into editable digital text, making it easier to capture, organize, and share ideas. Additional AI-powered capabilities include multilingual translation, text-to-speech functionality, and intuitive formatting tools such as text resizing, alignment controls, color customization, and shape editing. Users can also copy, duplicate, rearrange layers, lock or unlock objects, and delete content, enabling a more flexible and efficient collaborative workflow. The display also introduces AI-assisted search and learning capabilities that enrich meetings and classroom sessions. Users can search recognized text directly on YouTube and Wikipedia while generating explanations and learning challenges for educational content. Its Magical Pen transforms rough sketches into polished geometric shapes and converts simple drawings into detailed AI-generated visuals with multiple interpretations. Complementing this is the Shape Recognition feature, which automatically refines hand-drawn geometric figures into clean, professional-looking forms, making brainstorming sessions, presentations, and classroom interactions more engaging. Powered by Android 16 EDLA , the ViewBoard IN04V-N features a 4K Ultra HD display with an IR multi-touch frame, an RK3576 processor based on Arm Cortex-A72×4 and Cortex-A53×4 architecture, 8GB LPDDR5 RAM, and 128GB eMMC storage for responsive multitasking. Additional features include HDMI input supporting up to 3840 × 2160 at 60Hz, a Smart USB port compatible across all channels, 80-pin OPS support for 4K 60Hz display, integrated speakers, and NFC functionality for quick and secure access. With the launch of the ViewBoard new Series, ViewSonic continues to expand its portfolio of AI-enabled visual solutions that simplify collaboration while helping organizations create more connected, productive, and future-ready meeting and learning environments. The ViewBoard IN04V-N Series will be available across India through ViewSonic's authorized partners and enterprise sales network.

results.title: India News | Uttarakhand: Congress Leader Amar Mehta Dies at Bannu School Ground During Preparations for 'Chhatron Ki Goonj'
results.description: Get latest articles and stories on India at LatestLY. A Congress worker died after an iron cutter fell on his head during preparations for Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha Rahul Gandhi's "Chhatron Ki Goonj" programme at Bannu School Ground in Dehradun on Thursday, the Uttarakhand Pradesh Congress Committee (PCC) said.
